author: Bhavith S
tone: concerned, assertive, protective of retail interests
executive_summary: Retail investor Bhavith S strongly opposes rescinding Rule 611 (Order Protection Rule) under Regulation NMS, arguing that removing the Trade-Through Rule will expose retail investors to worse execution quality by enabling zero-commission brokerages to prioritize Payment for Order Flow revenue over best execution. The author draws on personal experience with execution discrepancies and analogizes the risk to unregulated cryptocurrency markets.

positions.best_execution: Opposes reliance on subjective best execution standard as a replacement for Rule 611; views it as insufficient protection for retail investors compared to a hard mechanical rule
positions.transparency: Implicitly supports price transparency through retention of NBBO-based trade-through protections

economic_arguments: Zero-commission brokerages have a financial incentive to route orders to venues that pay the highest PFOF rather than venues offering the best price for customers
Removing Rule 611 would allow brokerages to prioritize their own revenue (kickbacks) over customer execution quality
A subjective best execution standard is economically insufficient to replace a hard mathematical rule guaranteeing NBBO execution
Retail investors already experience execution discrepancies under current rules; rescission would worsen this
liquidity_arguments: No explicit liquidity arguments made; author implicitly assumes that NBBO-displayed prices represent the best available liquidity for retail investors
competition_arguments: Off-exchange wholesalers and alternative venues compete for order flow through PFOF arrangements rather than on price quality
Removing Rule 611 would tilt competitive dynamics further in favor of intermediaries over retail investors
Conflict of interest at zero-commission brokerages distorts competitive order routing away from best-price venues
retail_investor_arguments: Retail investors already experience price slippage and execution discrepancies even with Rule 611 in place
Cryptocurrency markets serve as a cautionary analogy: unregulated markets with no trade-through protection result in chronic slippage for everyday traders
Rule 611 is characterized as the primary safeguard preventing retail orders from being traded-through for the benefit of corporate middlemen
Retail investors cannot rely on broker discretion or subjective standards to protect their execution interests given inherent conflicts of interest
recommended_actions: Retain Rule 611 (Order Protection Rule) in its current form
Do not substitute the hard Trade-Through Rule with a subjective best execution standard
Protect retail investors from order routing practices that prioritize broker revenue over customer price improvement
key_quotes: I am deeply concerned that repealing Rule 611 will bring that same lack of strict price protection to the U.S. stock market, exacerbating the problem for everyday traders.

If the strict mandate to route orders to the venue displaying the National Best Bid and Offer (NBBO) is removed, these brokerages will be highly incentivized to route retail orders to favored off-exchange wholesalers and alternative venues that pay them the most in Payment for Order Flow (PFOF).
Without the rigid mechanical protection of the Trade-Through Rule, brokerages will prioritize their own kickbacks and revenue over securing the absolute best execution price for their customers.
The subjective 'best execution' standard is not a sufficient replacement for a hard, mathematical rule that guarantees retail investors get the best publicly displayed price.
Please do not remove the very safeguard that ensures retail trades are not traded-through for the benefit of corporate middlemen.
